title: Advanced Backlighting Makes This Projector Perfect for Summer Movie Nights
gist: Wired reviews the $5,998-$6,000 Hisense XR10 projector, an RGB-backlit, 6,000-lumen home cinema unit that exceeds the BT.2020 color gamut spec and rivals the pricier Xgimi Titan Noir Max, though it struggles in bright rooms and has weak voice control.

themes.theme: RGB backlighting migrates from TVs to projectors

insights.claim: RGB backlighting, the technology driving brighter and more colorful LED televisions, is now being adopted in high-end home projectors, with the Hisense XR10 as an example rated at 6,000 lumens and a 6,000:1 native contrast ratio.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Signals a technology diffusion pattern (RGB backlighting) moving from TV panel makers into the projector category, a potential upgrade cycle driver for display component suppliers.
insights.quote: RGB backlighting is the hottest new tech in the world of televisions, bringing brighter and more colorful images to the world of LED screens. And now, projectors are following suit.

insights.claim: The Hisense XR10 achieves 118 percent of the BT.2020 color gamut, exceeding the competing Xgimi Titan Noir Max's 110 percent, despite lacking the Titan's on-the-fly adjustable iris.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Shows two competing projector makers (Hisense, Xgimi) racing on color-gamut specs as a differentiator in the premium home cinema segment.
insights.quote: The XR10 hits 118 percent of the BT.2020 color gamut, which exceeds the popular Titan Noir Max (at 110 percent of the spec).

insights.claim: The Hisense XR10 performs well in real-world movie and gaming content but underperforms in bright-room daytime viewing, a limitation shared with other high-end projectors like the Leica Cine Play 1.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Bright-room performance remains an unsolved category-wide weakness for premium laser/LED projectors versus TVs, a persistent reason projectors haven't displaced TVs as the default living-room display.
insights.quote: One of the XR10's biggest struggles is with daytime viewing in a bright room.

insights.claim: The XR10's voice assistant, built on Hisense's VIDAA OS, is noticeably weaker than Amazon Alexa+ or Google Gemini implementations found on competing projectors, often misinterpreting basic commands.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Highlights a widening gap between proprietary smart-TV/projector OS voice assistants and the leading AI assistants (Gemini, Alexa+), reinforcing the value of third-party AI ecosystem integration for hardware vendors.
insights.quote: I prefer Alexa+ or Google Gemini implementations on projectors by far.
